coredata.dc:title: On-chip analysis of carbon dots effect on yeast replicative lifespan
coredata.dc:creator: Bagheri, Zeinab
Ehtesabi, Hamide
Hallaji, Zahra
Aminoroaya, Neda
Tavana, Hossein
Behroodi, Ebrahim
Rahimifard, Mahban
Abdollahi, Mohammad
Latifi, Hamid
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are promising nanomaterials for biosensing, bioimaging, and drug delivery due to their large surface area, excellent optical properties, and thermal and chemical stability. However, biosafety of CDs is still understudied, and there is not a generally accepted standard to evaluate the toxicity of CDs. We present a gradient network generator microfluidic device for dose-dependent testing of toxicity of CDs to a unicellular eukaryotic model organism, yeast Pichia pastoris. We fully characterized the microfluidic model and compare its performance with a conventional method. The gradient generator increased the contact area between the mixing species and enabled a high-throughput testing of CDs in a wide range of concentrations in cell chambers. Real time monitoring of yeast cell proliferation in the presence of CDs showed dose-dependent growth inhibition and various budding cell shape profiles. Comparing the result of microfluidic platform and conventional method revealed statistically significant differences in the proliferation rate of the cells between the two platforms. To understand the toxicity mechanism, we studied binding of CDs to P. pastoris and found increasing interactions of CDs with the cell surface at CDs larger concentrations. This study demonstrated the utility of the gradient generator microfluidic device as a convenient tool for toxicity assessment of nanomaterials at a cellular level.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Yeast Pichia pastoris
Microfluidics
Toxicity
Biosafety
Growth inhibition

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Yeast cell culture and interactions with CDs using well-plate assay
Simulation of mixing efficiency and shear stress in the microfluidic device
Yeast cell culture and interactions with CDs using well-plate assay
High-throughput measurements of yeast replicative lifespan in the CGG

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Iranian Cognitive Science and Technologies Council

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful to those who supported them especially Iranian Cognitive Science and Technologies Council.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The schematic shows the microfluidic channel network to generate linear concentrations of the test solution and the cell culture chambers, (b) mixing unit in the first stage, (c–e) Y-junction and the horizontal mixing unit in the cell culture chambers, and (f) the microfluidic chip with food coloring dyes added for visualization.

CDs characterization: (a) OD, PL, and PLE of CDs (inset: a solution of CDs under visible light and UV radiation), (b) emission of CDs at different excitation wavelengths, (c) DLS diagram, and (d) TEM image of prepared CDs.

Simulation of flow in the microfluidic device: (a) Outlet concentration is shown over a wide range of injection flow rates, (b) mixing efficiency at the end point of gradient generator module, (c) fluid velocity in cell chambers and their nearby microchannels. The side color bar shows the velocity range. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Calculating yeast cell number, based on proliferation assay using a conventional 96-well plate method and after 16 h of incubation with different concentrations of CDs. Data are shown as mean ± standard error of three different experiments (n = 3). * indicates significant difference from the control (no treatment) at p < 0.01.

(A) Solutions were collected from outlets using tips under visible light and UV radiation, (b) fluorescence intensity profiles of collected samples are shown. Inset shows comparison between simulation and experimental results.

(A) Light micrographs of cell chambers in different CDs concentrations from 0 mg/ml to 25 mg/ml (numbered 1 to 8). Scale bar is 10 μm. (b) Comparison of the yeast cell shape and cell size in each chamber treated with different CDs concentrations (2X magnified). (c) Calculated yeast cell number after 5 h of incubation with different CDs concentrations. Data are shown as mean ± standard error from three different experiments (n = 3). Significant difference from control was defined at p < 0.01.

Characterization of florescent CDs binding to Pichia pastoris. The curve illustrates the relative levels of fluorescence emission (excitation at 360 nm) induced by increasing concentrations of the CDs in the yeast cells suspension. Data are shown as mean ± standard error from three different experiments (n = 3). Significant difference from control was defined at **p < 0.001 and ***p < 0.0001.

Effect of different CDs concentrations on intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) of yeast cells. Data are shown as mean ± standard error from three different experiments (n = 3). * and ** indicate significant differences from the control (no treatment) at p < 0.01 and p < 0.001, respectively.

head.ce:title: On-chip analysis of carbon dots effect on yeast replicative lifespan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Life Sciences and Biotechnology, Shahid Beheshti University G.C., Velenjak, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Life Sciences and Biotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Institute of Pharmaceutical Sciences (TIPS), Department of Toxicology and Pharmacology, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tehran University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) are promising nanomaterials for biosensing, bioimaging, and drug delivery due to their large surface area, excellent optical properties, and thermal and chemical stability. However, biosafety of CDs is still understudied, and there is not a generally accepted standard to evaluate the toxicity of CDs. We present a gradient network generator microfluidic device for dose-dependent testing of toxicity of CDs to a unicellular eukaryotic model organism, yeast Pichia pastoris. We fully characterized the microfluidic model and compare its performance with a conventional method. The gradient generator increased the contact area between the mixing species and enabled a high-throughput testing of CDs in a wide range of concentrations in cell chambers. Real time monitoring of yeast cell proliferation in the presence of CDs showed dose-dependent growth inhibition and various budding cell shape profiles. Comparing the result of microfluidic platform and conventional method revealed statistically significant differences in the proliferation rate of the cells between the two platforms. To understand the toxicity mechanism, we studied binding of CDs to P. pastoris and found increasing interactions of CDs with the cell surface at CDs larger concentrations. This study demonstrated the utility of the gradient generator microfluidic device as a convenient tool for toxicity assessment of nanomaterials at a cellular level.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a member of the carbon nanomaterials family. They are smaller than 100 nm in size and 10 layers thick [1]. A large surface area, excellent thermal and physicochemical stability, and unique optical properties make CDs attractive for biological applications [2–10]. The preparation methods of CDs are relatively simple, low cost, and easily scalable. These include pyrolysis [11], oxidation techniques [12,13], hydrothermal techniques [14,15], electrochemical techniques [16], exfoliation-based techniques [17–19], and several others [9,20–23]. Although carbon is not an intrinsically toxic substance, the specific material and structural configuration of carbon nanomaterials may cause risks to human health. Several studies have evaluated toxicity of CDs in recent years [2,24–34]. However, relative to the rapid developments in applications of CDs, ecotoxicology and environmental hazard studies have fallen behind. Currently, little is known about toxic effects of CDs. Establishing new testing methods and technologies is expected to expedite toxicity analysis of CDs. New insights into potential hazardous effects of CDs in biological applications will lead to the development and design of safer materials.

The current approach of in vitro toxicity analysis is based on well-plate assays that require time-consuming manual handling of samples or costly automated robotic systems [35]. Microfluidic platforms are well suited for toxicity assessments due to their compatibility with high-throughput testing using small volumes of reagents, capability for parallel analyses, and ease of device fabrication. Several studies have used microfluidics for nanomaterials toxicity tests. Mahto et al. developed an integrated multi-compartment microfluidic device to evaluate toxicity of CdSe/ZnSe quantum dots (QDs) to fibroblast cells. Exposure of fibroblasts to QDs resulted in the production of reactive oxygen species and differences in cell morphology between static and flow conditions [36]. Kim et al. investigated cytotoxicity of mesoporous silica nanoparticles to endothelial cells lined on the surface of microchannels. Under flow and shear stress, the nanoparticles generated significant cytotoxicity [37]. Liu et al. developed a microfluidic device containing an embedded biocompatible scaffold to form hepatocyte spheroids and evaluate hepatotoxicity of silver nanoparticles. Treatment with the nanoparticles induced significantly greater membrane damage to the cells in the microfluidic device than in a conventional tissue culture plate [38].

Unicellular eukaryotic model organisms are widely used to study toxicity of chemicals because of similarities of their biochemical structure and cellular organization to those of higher organisms. Yeast cells have been used in microfluidic devices to study events such as aging, neurotoxicity of α-synuclein protein aggregation, cell segmentation and growth, cell trapping and biochemical analysis [25,39–42].

Concentration Gradient Generator (CGG) microfluidic devices offer a convenient platform to evaluate the toxicity of CDs to yeast cells. Due to the vast application of microfluidic gradient generators, various models have been introduced so far. In pressure driven models such as universal gradient generator, serial diluter, and tree-shaped gradient generator, mixing is performed through different mechanisms like parallel lamination, serial lamination and chaotic advection [43,44]. Some other models apply the active actuators such as micro pneumatic actuator, surface acoustic waves, and piezoelectric transducer to mix the solutions [45–47]. Tree-shaped gradient generator (also called Christmas tree) which was first proposed by the Whitesides group [48], is one of the most widely used models due to its simplicity in design, good uniformity, and robustness [49]. In Christmas tree model, two or more different solutions are infused into the inlets and a linear concentration gradient will be obtained at the outlets. Actually, the fluid streams of initial solutions are repeatedly split at the bifurcation points and then recombined and mixed in the mixer channels of next stages. In the serpentine mixer channel, molecular diffusion occurs across the interface between the laminar streams. Mixer channel length, solute diffusion coefficient and flow rate are the important variables to guarantee enough diffusive time for complete mixing at the end of each mixer branch to obtain a new uniformed concentration. Ultimately, the number of outlets solutions will be determined by the stages number and starting solutions number [48,49].

In this study, we applied a tree network microfluidic device for dose-dependent testing of CDs effect on various properties of a unicellular eukaryotic model organism, yeast Pichia pastoris. We synthesized blue emitting CDs through a simple, safe, and inexpensive method previously reported [50], and characterized them using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), dynamic light scattering (DLS), and spectrofluorometry. We optimized the geometry of the cell microchamber, integrated with a tree-shaped microchannels network, to decrease potential effects of shear stress on budding cells [41,51]. Using this device, we measured the proliferation rate and morphology of yeast cells in the presence of different concentrations of CDs, and binding of CDs to the cell surface. This study demonstrated the potential of the microfluidic model for high-throughput nanomaterial biocompatibility assays with the ability of dynamic sampling to overcome limitations of conventional methods. In addition, the large surface-area-to-volume ratios and the low flow shear stress in microchambers lead to the microenvironmental homogeneity. These unique features enable us to find out about the toxicity of new nanomaterials more precisely.

Anhydrous citric acid, sodium hydroxide, yeast extract, soy peptone, d-glucose, agar, quinine sulfate, sulfuric acid, and phosphate buffered saline (PBS) were purchased from Merck. 2′,7′ dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA), was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (GmbH Munich, Germany). The chemicals were all analytically pure and used as received. Milli–Q water was used to dilute samples to desired concentrations for experiments. SU-8 photoresist and polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) were obtained from MicroChem and Dow corning, respectively.

CDs were prepared using a citric acid pyrolysis method following our previous report [43]. Briefly, 1 gr of anhydrous citric acid was heated to 160 °C for 50 min and added to an NaOH aqueous solution (0.5 M) under continuous stirring. The sample solution was neutralized to pH of 7.0 with NaOH, and the stock solution of CDs was stored at 4 °C until use. Optical properties of the CDs solution were determined using ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy (LAMBDA 950 UV/Vis/NIR Spectrophotometer, PerkinElmer, USA). Photoluminescence (PL) and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra were obtained using a spectrophotometer (Varian Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer, Agilent, USA). Quantum yield (QY) of prepared CDs was measured using a quinine sulfate standard solution (quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.05 M solution of H2SO4). The particle size distribution of CDs was determined using dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Nanophox, Sympatec GmbH, Germany). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Zeiss – EM10C, Germany) was performed to analyze the morphology of CDs.

Standard photolithography was used to pattern the concentration gradient generator (CGG) design on a 20 μm-thick SU-8 2025 photoresist (MicroChem Corporation). The master mold was cast with a 10:1 mixture of PDMS:curing agent (Dow Corning Corp., USA) [52]. After degassing of the PDMS, it was cured at 90 °C for 30 min in an oven. The cured PDMS containing the design was peeled off from the master mold. The inlet and outlet reservoirs were punched at the ends of the microchannels using a biopsy punch of 1.25 mm diameter. The PDMS layer was bonded to a clean glass substrate using oxygen plasma [53].

Fig. 1 shows the design of the planar microfluidic device with two modules: a gradient-generating network (H1) and cell culture chamber module (H2), which their lengths are 14 and 10 mm, respectively. The gradient generator, which is a modified tree-shape network, consisted of six sinusoidal serpentine mixing stages. The length of L2 and L3 in the sinusoidal mixers are 380 and 1300 μm respectively. Using two inlet ports and six gradient generating stages resulted in eight linear concentrations at the outlet ports. Each culture chamber received an equal ratio mixture of the cell culture medium, which entered from the inlet port below the chambers, and the solution from the gradient module outlets. The width and the length (L4) of rectangular cell chamber are 480 and 650 μm, respectively.

To evaluate gradient generation in the CGG, stock solution of CDs and distilled water were infused through the two inlets, leading to eight linear concentrations of CDs solutions. From each outlet, 200 μL of the discharging solution was collected and the fluorescence intensity of the solution was recorded at excitation and emission wavelengths of 360 nm and 460 nm, respectively. The concentration of CDs of each sample was estimated based on a standard curve and compared with simulation results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Yeast cell culture and interactions with CDs using well-plate assay
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the inhibition of yeast cell growth by CDs, Pichia pastoris X33 wild type was grown on YPD agar plates with 1% (w/v) yeast extract, 2% (w/v) peptone, 2% (w/v) dextrose, and 2% (w/v) agar at 30 °C [53]. After 16 h of incubation, a colony was removed and added to sterilize YPD broth medium, and incubated at 30 °C and 200 rpm under aerobic conditions. Yeast growth was determined through measuring the optical density at 600 nm (OD600). Linear concentrations of CDs solutions were prepared in a concentration range produced by the microfluidic gradient generator. Yeast cell samples with an OD600 of 0.1 were treated with CDs solutions at 30 °C for 16 h. Finally, OD600 of the samples was measured.

Fabricated microfluidic devices were sterilized using collimated high power mercury light for 1 h. Just before use, the devices were placed in a vacuum chamber to prevent air bubbles formation in the sinusoidal serpentine channels. All solutions were degassed prior to use. CDs solution were prepared by mixing 2 ml of CDs stock solution and 2 ml of 2X fresh YPD broth medium. A CDs gradient was established using the YPD medium and the solutions supplied continuously at 5 μl/min from the two inlets. The YPD medium with P. pastoris were continuously infused into the microfluidic device from the third inlet at 10 μl/min (Fig. 1 (a)). The microbial growth was imaged inside the microchambers using an inverted light microscope (Olympus, Japan).

From the yeast grown using the above protocol, 500 μL of yeast culture medium at OD600 = 0.1 was added to 10 ml of 2X fresh YPD broth medium. Different concentrations of CDs (from 5 mg/ml to 25 mg/ml) in PBS (pH 7.4) were prepared and sterilized by UV radiation for 1 h before use. From each CDs solution, 10 ml was added into the yeast culture medium tubes. The yeast/CDs suspensions were kept at 30 °C for 3 h while slowly shaking. After incubation, the yeast cells were centrifuged for 5 min at 5000 rpm and washed twice with sterilized PBS to remove unbound CDs. The cell pellet was collected and resuspended in 3 ml of PBS buffer under gentle vortex. The emission intensity of cell-treated CDs was recorded with spectrofluorometer.

The intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) was determined using 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescin diacetate (DCFH-DA). The yeast cells were treated with different concentrations of CDs at 30 °C for 16 h in the next step, cells were gently homogenized in 20 ml of the extraction buffer and centrifuged. The supernatant was used for measuring ROS formation and protein content. To evaluate ROS production of the cells, 10 μl of DCFH-DA stock solution and 162 μl assay buffer were added to 50 μl of samples and incubated at 37 °C for 15 min. DCFH-DA deacetylated and converted to DCFH by cellular esterase. Then, cytosolic ROS oxidized this compound to 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein (DCF). Finally, the changes in emission were detected by 96-well microplate fluorometer every 1 min up to 60 min at 488 nm excitation and 525 nm emission [54]. Furthermore, protein content of the samples was investigated using Bradford reagent.

At least three independent experiments were carried out for each condition. Data were presented as mean ± SEM. One-way ANOVA and Tukey's multi-comparison tests were carried out to evaluate statistical differences. The p-value of 0.05 was considered significant [55].

Fig. 2 (a) shows the OD, photoluminescence (PL), and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) of the synthesized CDs. The inset illustrates the solution of CDs under visible light and UV radiation. The peak wavelengths for excitation and emission spectra were at 360 nm and 460 nm, respectively. Measurement of the PL spectra in a wide range of wavelengths showed that the excitation and emission wavelengths were independent (Fig. 2(b)). DLS measurements showed a mean diameter of 3 ± 0.4 nm for the CDs (Fig. 2(c)). The TEM image showed a uniform size distribution of CDs (Fig. 2(d)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simulation of mixing efficiency and shear stress in the microfluidic device
ce:section.ce:para:
The performance mixing units in CGG was quantified by calculating the variance of the mixture concentration in a cross section of the mixing channel perpendicular to the flow direction using:
(1)
σ =
1
N − 1
∑
i = 1
N
(
X i
−
X ¯
i
)
2
,
(2)
X ¯
i
=
∑
i = 1
n
X i
N
,
(3)
M = 1 −
σ 2
σ
max
2
,

In Eqs (1)–(3), σ represents standard deviation,
X i
denotes normalized color intensity,
X ¯
i
represents average color intensity, and
σ
m a x
is the maximum standard deviation at the inlet. M is the mixing efficiency [53]. Fig. 3 (a) shows that the concentration of CDs at each outlet of the gradient generator module is uniform and different from other outlets in a 0–100 μl/min range of injection flow rates. Fig. 3(b) shows the mixing efficiency at the end point of gradient generator module. The mixing efficiency decreased by increase in the flow rate due to reduced time of diffusion, but remained as high as ∼95–97% in the second to seventh channels This demonstrates the effectiveness of the tree network design for mixing of solutions in microchannels.

Fluid shear stress in the microchannel can disturb the budding process in the yeast cell and cause the cell morphology variation [41,51]. In this regard, the chamber module of the microfluidic device was designed to confine the cells to a no-flow condition and avoid shear stress on yeast cells. In this design, the closure of the cell chamber causes the maximum amount of fluid to flow through the main channels and does not perfuse into the chamber due to the convection transport, which provides a condition to isolate cell proliferation chamber form bulk fluid flow. According to Fig. 3 (c), the flow velocity of 400 μm/s in the microchannels decreased to approximately zero in cell chambers. Owing to a positive correlation between velocity and shear stress, a static growth condition is formed in microchamber to facilitate suitable condition for budding yeast cells. In this situation, the exchange between the rectangular cell chamber is supported mainly by diffusive transport of nutrients into the chambers, which is enough for the refreshment of the yeast cell microenvironment [56].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Yeast cell culture and interactions with CDs using well-plate assay
ce:section.ce:para: To measure the effect of CDs concentration on the yeast cells proliferation, cells were exposed to a linear concentration of CDs (0–25 mg/ml) and the growth inhibitory effect of CDs was determined using OD measurements (Fig. 4 ). Measurements showed no significant cell growth inhibition at concentrations of CDs lower than and equal to 7.7 mg/ml. CDs at concentrations higher than 7.7 mg/ml reduced cell proliferation dose-dependently (p < 0.01). The largest decrease in the yeast cell proliferation occurred at ∼25 mg/ml concentration of CDs at which cell proliferation was 60 ± 5% of the negative control sample (no treatment).

Recent studies have shown that CDs exhibit cytotoxicity toward a variety of human cells and bacteria [31,57]. Our result establishes CDs toxicity to yeast cells and demonstrates their significantly lower toxicity than graphene oxide nanomaterials that were toxic to yeast cells at concentrations higher than 0.5 mg/ml [58]. Additionally, the CDs toxicity is significantly lower than QDs that exhibited marked toxicity to yeast cells at concentrations as low as 17.07 nM [59]. The toxicity was potentially because yeast cells internalize QDs of 4.1 nm–5.8 nm, suggesting that care must be taken for the design and development of aqueous QDs for biological applications. Low toxicity of CDs even at high concentrations makes them promising materials for biological applications.

The performance of the CGG was evaluated using computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulations prior to experiments with CDs. A series of solutions with different concentrations of CDs were formed at the CGG and samples were collected (Fig. 5 (a)). The measured fluorescence intensity of the samples is shown in Fig. 5(b). The concentration of collected samples was quantified using a standard curve. Results were in close agreement with the computational simulation simulations (inset in Fig. 5(b)), indicating the reliability of experimental approach to generate gradients of aqueous solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: High-throughput measurements of yeast replicative lifespan in the CGG
ce:section.ce:para: Next, we studied the growth rate and morphology of yeast cells under different conditions using the microfluidic device. Light micrographs of cell culture chambers in 0 mg/ml to 25 mg/ml concentrations of CDs after 5 h of incubation are shown in Fig. 6 (a). The morphology of yeast cells significantly changed at larger concentrations of CDs (Fig 6(b)). While at low concentrations there were more aged mother cells and proliferative cells, there were more Schmoo cells and stationary phase cells at higher CDs concentrations. We used image processing and analysis in ImageJ to calculate the number of yeast cells in each chamber. The results are shown in Fig. 6(c). Increasing the CDs concentration dose-dependently decreased the number of yeast cells. Decrease in cell number was fairly linear and at the largest CDs concentration of 25 mg/ml, there was ∼94% fewer cells than in control condition. In contrast to results from the microfluidic device, there were ∼60% viable cells at the 25 mg/ml concentration of CDs with well plate method which require further studies to investigate the reasons underlying this difference.

To elucidate interactions of yeast cells with CDs, we quantitatively studied binding of CDs to the surface of the cells. Fig. 7 shows the CDs intensity emission at 460 nm. Increasing the CDs concentration increased the emitted light intensity, indicating binding of CDs to the surface of yeast cells. CDs binding to the surface of yeast cells may induce toxicity [60]. Additionally, the ability of CDs to stimulate production of intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) beyond the capacity of cells to neutralize them may also be responsible for the growth inhibition of CDs at higher concentrations [3,60,61].

Administration of CDs in all concentrations showed considerable increment in cytosolic ROS level (Fig. 8 ). At 15 and 25 mg/ml of CDs, fulfilling increase of ROS was detected compared to the control group (P < 0.001). As demonstrated, low concentration of CDs has also caused a noticeable change in ROS level of the cells (P < 0.05). The ability of CDs to stimulate production of intracellular ROS beyond the capacity of cells to neutralize them may also be responsible for the growth inhibition of CDs at higher concentrations [3,60,61].

We presented a microfluidic device to generate a network of gradients of carbon dots (CDs) and enable high-throughput testing of CDs effect on the proliferation of budding yeast cells maintained in integrated cell chambers. We used the gradient generator module to form eight linear concentrations of CDs in the 0–25 mg/ml range and evaluated the response of the yeast cells. Increasing the CDs concentration dose-dependently decreased the number of yeast cells. While 60% cell proliferation was measured with well plate method at 25 mg/ml of CDs, the same concentration resulted in ∼6% proliferative cells in the microfluidic device and significantly altered morphology of the yeast cells. Our measurements showed that interactions of CDs with the yeast cell surface increases approximately linearly with increase in the CDs concentrations. This potentially inhibits the growth of yeast cells through stimulation of cells to produce ROS at toxic levels. This study demonstrated the potential of the microfluidic model for high-throughput nanomaterial biocompatibility assays with the ability of dynamic sampling to overcome limitations of conventional methods.
There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to those who supported them especially Iranian Cognitive Science and Technologies Council.
